reference: The text references reports detailing the diagnostic and therapeutic problems encountered in cases of tuberculous peritonitis. These challenges may include difficulty in obtaining a definitive diagnosis due to nonspecific symptoms and the need for advanced imaging or invasive procedures to confirm the presence of tuberculosis in the peritoneal cavity. Additionally, treatment may be complicated by the need for prolonged antituberculous therapy and potential complications arising from the disease itself.

reference: When deciding to perform a sputum culture for MRSA or P. aeruginosa in patients with community-acquired pneumonia, clinicians should consider prior infection with either pathogen as a strong risk factor. Additionally, recent hospitalization and treatment with parenteral antibiotics within the last 90 days are associated with an increased risk of these pathogens. Although the recommendations are not based on high-grade evidence, they aim to enhance antibiotic use and improve understanding of local pathogen prevalences and resistance patterns.

response:
### Clinician Summary
Bedaquiline is part of a new 6–9-month regimen (BPaL) conditionally recommended for patients with MDR/RR-TB and additional fluoroquinolone resistance, under operational research conditions. [2]. The use of a shorter all-oral bedaquiline-containing regimen in patients with MDR/RR-TB was associated with higher treatment success rates compared to standardized shorter regimens. [7]. Bedaquiline is included in recommendations for longer regimens for MDR/RR-TB, ensuring that treatment starts with at least four TB agents likely to be effective. [8].

New recommendations have been made regarding the use of bedaquiline beyond 6 months and in combination with delamanid. [3].

### Patient Summary
A new treatment option that includes bedaquiline has been recommended for patients with a specific type of drug-resistant TB. [2]. Patients using a shorter treatment that includes bedaquiline have shown better recovery rates compared to other treatments. [7]. Bedaquiline is an important part of longer treatment plans for drug-resistant TB to help ensure effective care. [8].

There are updated guidelines on how long bedaquiline can be used and how it can be safely combined with other medications. [3].

### References
1. KB: Existing KB - Guidelines, Document: Unknown
reference: Bedaquiline is significant in the treatment of MDR/RR-TB patients as it is included in an all-oral treatment regimen that lasts 9–12 months, which has been shown to improve patient outcomes compared to other regimens that conform to WHO guidelines. The South African Department of Health has phased in injectable-free regimens since 2017, replacing injectables with bedaquiline, indicating a shift towards safer treatment options. However, the evidence supporting the effectiveness of these shorter regimens primarily comes from programmatic data, highlighting the need for further research to validate these findings.

### Clinician Summary
Comorbidities represent important prognostic factors and contribute to the severity index in community-acquired pneumonia (CAP). [1]. Severe CAP frequently develops in individuals with comorbid factors such as underlying cardiopulmonary disease, diminished splenic function, and/or heightened pathogenic virulence. [2]. Negative prognostic factors in CAP include preexisting lung disease, underlying cardiac disease, poor splenic function, advanced age, multilobar involvement, past infection with tuberculosis, and delayed initiation of appropriate antimicrobial therapy. [5].

Comorbid conditions affect a patient’s risk of contracting CAP caused by MRSA, Pseudomonas, or other antibiotic-resistant pathogens. [6].

### Patient Summary
Having other health issues can make pneumonia more severe and harder to treat. [1]. People with conditions like heart or lung disease are more likely to get severe pneumonia. [2]. Certain health problems can increase the risk of complications from pneumonia. [5].

Other health conditions can also increase the chances of getting pneumonia from resistant bacteria. [6].

### References
1. KB: Existing KB - Guidelines, Document: Medscape CAP Clinical Practice Guidelines
2. KB: Existing KB - Guidelines, Document: BTS Pneumonia in Adults Guidelines (UK)
reference: Comorbid conditions significantly increase a patient's risk of contracting CAP caused by antibiotic-resistant pathogens such as MRSA and Pseudomonas. This necessitates a more aggressive management approach, including potential hospitalization and the consideration of broader-spectrum antibiotics. Clinicians must evaluate these comorbidities when determining the appropriate treatment plan and monitoring strategies for patients with CAP.

response:
### Clinician Summary
Multidisciplinary TB teams manage all cases of active TB in liaison with prisons, custody suites, or immigration removal centre healthcare providers. [1]. They coordinate the care of under-served people and those with active TB who receive enhanced case management. [2]. Multidisciplinary TB teams should aim to find people with active TB who are lost to follow-up or who stop using services before completing diagnostic investigations. [4].

They should implement strategies for active and latent TB to encourage people to follow the treatment plan and prevent people from stopping treatment early. [5]. Multidisciplinary TB teams should provide all TB services and ensure that local strategy and service commissioning focuses on an end-to-end pathway. [8]. They should consider using peer educators to promote the uptake of TB screening in hostels and day centres. [10].

### Patient Summary
A team of professionals works together to manage your TB care, especially if you have complex health or social issues. [9]. They will help you stay on track with your treatment and find you if you miss appointments. [4]. They will also work to make sure you get the right support and information about TB. [5].

### References
1. KB: Existing KB - Guidelines, Document: NICE TB Guidelines NG33 - Recommendations (UK 2024)
reference: Multidisciplinary TB teams are tasked with locating individuals who have active tuberculosis (TB) but have become lost to follow-up or have discontinued their engagement with healthcare services before completing necessary diagnostic investigations. This proactive approach is essential to ensure that patients receive timely diagnosis and treatment, thereby reducing the risk of further transmission of TB within the community.

reference: The severity of a patient's condition is assessed using the Glasgow Coma Scale (GCS) by categorizing the score into three stages. Stage 1 is characterized by a GCS of 15, indicating the patient is alert and oriented without any focal neurological deficits. Stage 2 includes a GCS ranging from 14 to 11, or a score of 15 with the presence of focal neurological deficits. Stage 3 is defined by a GCS of 10 or less, which may occur with or without focal neurological deficits.

response:
### Clinician Summary
Work with a specialist multidisciplinary team with experience of managing TB and liver disease if a person has severe liver disease, for example, Child‑Pugh level B or C. [1]. Select a treatment regimen based on coexisting medical conditions and potential for drug-drug interactions. [2]. Manage treatment with caution and ensure careful monitoring of liver function in patients with severe liver disease. [5].

Select regimens with fewer potentially hepatotoxic agents in patients with advanced liver disease or elevated serum ALT. [6]. Consider the increased likelihood of drug-induced hepatitis in patients with preexisting advanced liver disease. [7]. Expert consultation is advisable, and adjustments during treatment may be necessary. [8]. response:
### Clinician Summary
We suggest initial adjunctive corticosteroid therapy not be routinely used in patients with tuberculous pericarditis (conditional recommendation; very low certainty in the evidence). [3]. A recent placebo-controlled randomized clinical trial with 1400 participants did not find a difference in the combined primary endpoint of the trial, which included mortality, cardiac tamponade, or constrictive pericarditis, between patients treated with adjunctive corticosteroids vs placebo. [5]. Therefore, we suggest that adjunctive corticosteroids should not be used routinely in the treatment of patients with pericardial tuberculosis. [10].

### Patient Summary
Doctors suggest that corticosteroids should not be used regularly for treating tuberculous pericarditis because recent studies did not show benefits. [3]. A large study found no difference in important health outcomes between patients who received corticosteroids and those who did not. [5]. As a result, doctors recommend against the routine use of corticosteroids for this condition. [10].

reference: Adjunctive corticosteroids are utilized in the management of tuberculous pericarditis to reduce inflammation and improve clinical outcomes. The experience reported by Burgess et al. suggests that corticosteroids may play a beneficial role in this condition, potentially alleviating symptoms and preventing complications associated with pericardial inflammation. However, the specific dosing and duration of corticosteroid therapy should be guided by clinical judgment and individual patient factors.

response:
### Clinician Summary
Directly observed therapy (DOT) is recommended as the standard of care for patients with TB disease, and it involves a health care worker observing the patient ingest medications, monitoring for adverse events, and providing social support. [2]. Consideration of patient-specific factors such as risk factors for poor adherence, work or family responsibilities, and the need for social support is essential when selecting treatment administration options. [5]. Additional supervision of local supporters or health care workers is necessary, as family members cannot be relied upon as the sole option for care. [5].

A package of treatment adherence interventions, including social and psychological support, should be offered in conjunction with the selected treatment administration option. [4]. Community- or home-based DOT is preferred over health facility-based DOT or unsupervised treatment, and trained lay providers or health care workers should administer DOT rather than family members. [10].

### Patient Summary
Your health care worker will watch you take your TB medications and help you with any side effects while also providing support. [2]. It's important to consider your personal situation, like your job or family, when deciding how you will take your TB treatment. [5]. You may need extra help from health care workers, not just family, to make sure you get the care you need. [5].

You might receive additional support like food or transportation to help you stick to your treatment plan. [4]. It's better to have your treatment at home or in the community rather than at a health facility, and trained health workers should help you instead of just family members. [10].

reference: When implementing Directly Observed Therapy (DOT) for tuberculosis treatment, it is essential to make decisions in concert with the patient to ensure their cooperation and adherence. DOT can be administered in various settings, including the office, clinic, or the patient's home, workplace, or school, provided that the location is mutually agreeable. This flexibility in the administration of DOT allows for personalized care and can enhance treatment compliance.

### Clinician Summary
Key considerations when developing a case management plan include improving treatment literacy, discussing expected outcomes, reviewing methods of supervision, and discussing infectiousness and infection control measures. [1]. Health care providers should choose the appropriate treatment regimen based on drug-susceptibility results, coexisting medical conditions, and potential for drug-drug interactions. [2]. Patient factors should be considered when selecting administration schedule and regimen composition. [3].

The GDG considered values and preferences, including equity, acceptability, and feasibility, in addition to clinical outcomes and potential risks of increasing toxicities. [4]. The most acceptable regimen is one that has few to no side effects, is short, and is all-oral, with added value given to a low pill burden. [5].

### Patient Summary
It's important to understand your treatment and its possible side effects. [1]. Your health care provider will consider your specific health needs and any other medications you are taking when choosing your treatment. [2]. The way your treatment is given may be adjusted based on your personal situation. [3].

Your preferences about treatment, including how it affects your daily life, are important in deciding on the best regimen. [4]. Patients prefer treatments that are easy to take, have fewer side effects, and are shorter in duration. [5].

The qualitative study highlighted the importance of aligning treatment options with patient values and preferences to enhance adherence and overall satisfaction.

[1]. Patients with multidrug-resistant TB (MDR-TB) should be treated using mainly ambulatory care rather than models of care based principally on hospitalization. [2]. Ambulatory care is recommended over hospitalization models for patients on MDR-TB treatment. [3].

The cost per disability adjusted life year (DALY) averted was lower under outpatient-based care than under inpatient-based care in the vast majority of settings for which cost–effectiveness was modelled. [9].

### Patient Summary
Using an ambulatory model for treating MDR-TB can save money and avoid as many deaths as hospitalization. [1]. It's better for patients with MDR-TB to be treated mainly in outpatient settings instead of hospitals. [2]. Ambulatory care is preferred over hospitalization for treating MDR-TB. [3].

In most cases, outpatient care is cheaper and more effective than inpatient care for treating MDR-TB. [9].

It has been shown to avoid at least as many deaths among primary and secondary cases compared to hospitalization. Additionally, clinic-based ambulatory treatment may be further enhanced by home-based ambulatory care, which can provide even greater cost-effectiveness, provided that proper infection control measures are implemented to minimize transmission risks.

The radiographic presentation of primary tuberculosis in children is characterized by intrathoracic lymphadenopathy with or without lung opacities, occasionally presenting with lymph node enlargement that compresses airways, with or without hyperinflation or collapse of lobe or lung; breakthrough of node(s) in the airways can manifest with lobar or segmental infiltration, and a miliary pattern. Clinically, this can lead to significant lymph node enlargement that may compress the airways, potentially resulting in hyperinflation or collapse of a lobe or lung. Additionally, if lymph nodes erode into the airways, it can manifest as lobar or segmental infiltration, or even present with a miliary pattern.

reference: Levofloxacin's cavitary penetration is significant in the treatment of multidrug-resistant tuberculosis (MDR-TB) as it may enhance the drug's efficacy in reaching and eradicating bacteria within pulmonary cavities. Effective penetration of antibiotics into cavitary lesions is crucial for achieving successful treatment outcomes in patients with MDR-TB, where standard therapies may fail. response:
### Clinician Summary
Patient factors, such as age, comorbid conditions, immunologic competence, nutritional status, and alcohol abuse, can contribute to treatment failure in tuberculosis. [1]. Radiographic features, such as extent of disease and presence and size of cavities, are associated with treatment failure. [1]. Microbiologic factors, including baseline colony count and culture positivity at 2 or 3 months, can lead to treatment failure. [1].

Genetic factors, including individual genetic features of drug absorption and metabolism, individual vulnerability to toxicities, and immunologic characteristics, may contribute to treatment failure. [1]. Programmatic factors, such as adherence support interventions, dosing frequency, and monitoring, are associated with treatment failure. [1]. Pharmacokinetic factors, including absorption, metabolism, protein binding, drug clearance, and total drug quantities administered, can influence treatment failure. [1]. Bacillary factors, such as drug tolerance and strain susceptibilities to drugs in the regimen, are linked to treatment failure. [1]. Regimen factors, including the number of active drugs, bactericidal and sterilizing potency, synergy or antagonism, and duration of therapy, can contribute to treatment failure. [1].

### Patient Summary
Factors like your age, other health conditions, how well your immune system works, your nutrition, and alcohol use can affect your tuberculosis treatment. [1]. The extent of your disease and the presence of cavities in your lungs can impact treatment success. [1]. How many bacteria are in your body at the start and whether tests show bacteria after 2 or 3 months can influence treatment outcomes. [1].

Your genetic makeup can affect how your body processes tuberculosis drugs and how you respond to treatment. [1]. Support for sticking to your treatment plan and how often you take your medicine can affect whether treatment works. [1]. How your body absorbs and clears the tuberculosis drugs can play a role in treatment success. [1]. The type of tuberculosis bacteria you have and how they respond to drugs can affect treatment outcomes. [1]. The treatment plan, including how many drugs you take and how long you take them, can influence your chances of success. [1].

### References
1. KB: Existing KB - Guidelines, Document: ATS CDC IDSA Drug-Susceptible TB Treatment Guidelines 2016
2. KB: Existing KB - Guidelines, Document: Unknown
3. KB: Existing KB - Guidelines, Document: Medscape CAP Clinical Practice Guidelines
reference: Malabsorption of antimycobacterial drugs is identified as a potential cause of treatment failure in tuberculosis. This suggests that the effective absorption of these medications is critical for achieving successful treatment outcomes. Clinicians should consider evaluating patients for malabsorption issues if treatment failure occurs.

### Clinician Summary
The likelihood of drug-induced hepatitis is increased with prior advanced liver disease, liver transplant, or hepatitis C infection. [1]. Expert consultation is advisable, and adjustments during treatment may be necessary. [2]. Careful monitoring of liver function is essential, especially in people with non-severe liver disease or abnormal liver function before starting treatment. [7].

Regimens with fewer potentially hepatotoxic agents should be selected in patients with advanced liver disease or whose serum ALT is greater than three times the upper limit of normal at baseline. [10].

### Patient Summary
If you have liver disease, the risk of liver damage from tuberculosis medications is higher. [1]. It's important to get expert advice and possibly adjust your treatment. [2]. Your liver function will need to be monitored closely during treatment. [7].

Doctors may choose medications that are less harmful to the liver if your liver is severely affected. [10].

### Clinician Summary
Key components of an individualized case management plan include setting up patient reminders and systems to follow-up missed appointments, use of incentives and enablers, field and home visits, and integration and coordination of tuberculosis care with the patient’s primary and specialty care. [1]. Improving treatment literacy by educating the patient about tuberculosis and its treatment, discussing expected outcomes of treatment, reviewing methods of supervision and assessing response to therapy, and discussing infectiousness and infection control measures are also key considerations. [4]. The TB case manager should offer a risk assessment, educate the person about TB and the treatment, develop an individual care plan after discussion with the person, gain the person's consent to the plan, coordinate discharge planning, involve representatives from other allied professions, and explore ways that peers and voluntary organizations can provide support. [7].

### Patient Summary
Your case management plan will include reminders for your appointments, support to help you stay on track with your treatment, and visits to your home if needed. [1]. You will receive education about tuberculosis and its treatment, and we will discuss what to expect from your treatment and how to stay safe from spreading the infection. [4]. Your case manager will assess your needs, help create a personalized care plan, and involve other professionals to support you. [7].

reference: An individualized case management plan should include interventions tailored to address the specific needs and barriers faced by the patient. This plan must be periodically reviewed and revised in collaboration with the patient and medical team to assess the clinical response to therapy. Additionally, it should involve monitoring for potential drug toxicities and addressing any challenges related to treatment adherence.

Consider a single interferon‑gamma release assay for people aged 18 to 65 years. [1]. Offer Mantoux testing to diagnose latent TB in adults aged 18 to 65 who are close contacts of a person with pulmonary or laryngeal TB. [2]. For adults aged 18 to 65, a blood test called an interferon-gamma release assay is recommended for screening latent tuberculosis. [1]. If you have been in close contact with someone who has a specific type of TB, a skin test called the Mantoux test may be used. [2]. If the skin test isn't available, the blood test can be used instead. [7].

### Clinician Summary
AFB smear positive but culture-negative sputa in patients with far-advanced cavitary tuberculosis during treatment is thought to contain dead organisms and is not a sign of treatment failure. [1]. This occurrence is most frequent after the first few months of treatment. [2].

### Patient Summary
If a patient with advanced tuberculosis has a positive AFB smear but a negative culture, it likely means there are dead germs present, and this does not mean the treatment is failing. [1].

### References
1. KB: Existing KB - Guidelines, Document: ATS CDC IDSA Drug-Susceptible TB Treatment Guidelines 2016
reference: The presence of AFB smear positive but culture-negative sputa in patients with far-advanced cavitary tuberculosis after the first few months of treatment indicates that the organisms detected are likely dead. This finding is not considered a sign of treatment failure, as it reflects the remnants of dead organisms rather than active disease. Therefore, clinicians should interpret these results with caution and continue to monitor the patient's clinical status and response to therapy.

### Clinician Summary
Prison and immigration removal centre healthcare services should liaise with the named TB case manager to ensure contingency plans for continuation of treatment are drawn up for prisoners and immigration removal centre detainees with TB. [1]. Multidisciplinary TB teams, in liaison with prisons, custody suites or immigration removal centre healthcare providers, should manage all cases of active TB. Investigations and follow-up should be undertaken within the prison or immigration removal centre if possible. [6]. Prisons and immigration removal centres should ensure multidisciplinary TB staff have access to prisoners and detainees who need treatment. [9].

Healthcare professionals in prisons and immigration removal centres should ensure prisoners and detainees are screened for TB within 48 hours of arrival. [12].

### Patient Summary
Healthcare services in prisons and immigration removal centres work with TB case managers to make sure treatment continues for detainees with TB. [1]. TB cases are managed by specialized teams within the prison or immigration centre, and they check on patients regularly. [6]. Prison healthcare staff will have access to detainees who need TB treatment. [9].

user_input: What methodology was used to develop the recommendations for the programmatic management of drug-resistant tuberculosis, and how does it ensure the quality of evidence and strength of recommendations?

['In this updated 
document, stakeholders will be able to distinguish between previous recommendations that remain 
valid, those that have been updated, and those that have been newly developed based on additional

tuberculosis, and are primarily intended for use by national TB control programmes (NTPs), public 
health agencies, and other key constituencies involved in the planning, implementation and monitoring 
of activities for the programmatic management of drug-resistant TB. The methods used to develop and formulate the recommendations complied with WHO standards 
for guideline development, and were based on up-to-date evidence reviews, complemented with 
additional information on values and preferences, feasibility and acceptability, and cost. The GRADE

guidelines on drug-resistant tuberculosis treatment that were published in 2019.9

and care. The recommendations are presented below.', 'More recently, WHO has started to consolidate guidelines, in response to requests from Member

### Clinician Summary
People with latent TB infection are infected with TB bacteria, but they do not have TB disease. However, if these bacteria become active and multiply, latent TB infection can develop into TB disease. Treating people with latent TB infection substantially reduces the risk that latent TB infection will progress to TB disease. [1]. Progression from untreated latent TB infection to TB disease accounts for approximately 80% of U.S. TB cases. Treating latent TB infection is 90% effective in preventing the development of TB disease. [7].

### Patient Summary
If you have latent TB infection, you have TB bacteria in your body but are not sick. If not treated, it can turn into TB disease, which is serious. Treatment can help prevent this from happening. [1].

The significance of LTBI lies in the potential for the bacteria to become active and multiply, leading to the development of tuberculosis disease. Therefore, individuals with LTBI are at risk for progression to active TB, particularly if they have compromised immune systems or other risk factors.

response:
### Clinician Summary
MRSA community-acquired pneumonia (CAP) is significantly associated with prior long-term hemodialysis use and carries a higher inpatient mortality rate (13.3%) than all-cause non–S aureus CAP (2%) [1]. Risk factors for MRSA pneumonia include prior hospital admission of 2 or more days in the preceding 90 days, antibiotics in the preceding 90 days, residence in a long-term care facility, septic shock at the time of CAP, immunosuppression, enteral tube feedings, nonambulatory status, gastric acid suppression, chronic hemodialysis, congestive heart failure, and history of MRSA colonization [2]. Negative prognostic factors in community-acquired pneumonia (CAP) include preexisting lung disease, underlying cardiac disease, poor splenic function, advanced age, multilobar involvement, past infection with tuberculosis, and delayed initiation of appropriate antimicrobial therapy [3].

Morbidity and mortality associated with CAP are most common in elderly patients and immunocompromised hosts, with significant comorbidities such as structural lung disease or cancer, and presenting signs of severe infection such as an increased respiratory rate, hypotension, fever, multilobar involvement, anemia, and hypoxia [4].

### Patient Summary
Patients with MRSA pneumonia who have had long-term hemodialysis are at a higher risk of dying in the hospital [1]. Factors that increase the risk of MRSA pneumonia include recent hospital stays, recent antibiotic use, living in a long-term care facility, being in septic shock, having a weakened immune system, needing tube feedings, not being able to walk, taking medications that reduce stomach acid, having chronic kidney disease, heart failure, and a history of MRSA infections [2]. Older patients and those with weakened immune systems are more likely to have serious problems or die from pneumonia, especially if they have other health issues or show severe symptoms like fast breathing, low blood pressure, fever, multiple areas of lung infection, low red blood cell counts, or low oxygen levels [4].

2. KB: Existing KB - Guidelines, Document: Unknown
reference: Patients with MRSA community-acquired pneumonia (CAP) who have a history of prior long-term hemodialysis use are significantly associated with a higher inpatient mortality rate. Specifically, the mortality rate for MRSA CAP is reported to be 13.3%, which is notably higher than the 2% mortality rate associated with all-cause non-S. aureus CAP. This highlights the importance of recognizing underlying health conditions that may exacerbate the severity of MRSA CAP.

response:
### Clinician Summary
Continuity of care is essential to ensure that prisoners on TB treatment are properly managed during transfers or releases, involving planning with other agencies. [1]. Prison health services must have arrangements in place to ensure continuity of care for prisoners on TB treatment during transfers or releases. [2]. Named TB case managers are responsible for contingency planning for the discharge of prisoners on TB treatment, ensuring continuity of treatment. [3].

Healthcare professionals should inquire about TB medication upon arrival at a facility to ensure continuity of treatment. [5]. Patients who move between jurisdictions while being treated for TB are more likely to be lost to follow-up, highlighting the importance of continuity of care. [6]. Multidisciplinary TB teams should arrange directly observed therapy for prisoners after their release to maintain continuity of care. [7]. Healthcare services should collaborate with TB case managers to create contingency plans for the continuation of treatment for TB patients. [8].

### Patient Summary
It's important to plan for care before a prisoner on TB treatment is moved or released, involving other agencies. [1]. Prison health services need to have plans to keep care going for prisoners on TB treatment when they are moved or released. [2]. A named TB case manager helps make sure that prisoners on TB treatment have a plan for their care when they leave prison. [3].

When prisoners arrive at a new facility, healthcare workers should ask if they are taking TB medication to keep their treatment going. [5]. Patients moving between areas while being treated for TB are more likely to miss follow-up care, showing why continuity is important. [6]. After release, teams should set up therapy for prisoners to ensure they continue their TB treatment. [7]. Healthcare services should work with TB case managers to make sure there are plans for continuing treatment for TB patients. [8].

response:
### Clinician Summary
Factors to consider include previous treatment history, baseline resistance patterns, site of disease, and age. [1]. Treatment can be individualized based on disease severity, organs involved, and response to treatment. [3]. In elderly patients with high bacillary burden, the benefits and risks of extending treatment duration should be carefully considered. [5].

The duration of treatment may be modified according to the patient's response to therapy. [8]. The risk of recurrence is lower when the continuation phase of treatment is extended. [10].

### Patient Summary
Doctors consider your past treatments, how resistant the bacteria are, where the disease is in your body, and your age when deciding how long to treat tuberculosis. [1]. Your treatment may be adjusted based on how severe your disease is and how well you respond to the treatment. [3]. For older patients with severe disease, doctors weigh the pros and cons of extending treatment duration. [5].

The length of treatment might change depending on how well you are doing with the therapy. [8]. If treatment continues longer, the chance of the disease coming back is lower. [10].

reference: The optimal duration of treatment for tuberculosis should take into account several key factors, including the patient's previous treatment history, baseline resistance patterns, the site of disease, and the patient's age. These factors help tailor the treatment regimen to maximize efficacy while minimizing potential harms. Additionally, employing risk-stratification approaches can further optimize the balance of benefits versus risks associated with the duration of therapy.

response:
### Clinician Summary
Patient involvement is crucial in making decisions concerning treatment supervision and overall care, which helps achieve adherence and balances patient rights with public safety. [1]. Effective patient support is important to enable adherence to treatment, which is critical for both the patient and public health. [2]. Improving treatment literacy and discussing expected outcomes with patients are key considerations in developing a case management plan to ensure adherence. [4].

Patient-centered approaches in tuberculosis management emphasize the importance of involving patients in their care. [5]. Higher rates of treatment success and adherence are associated with patient support interventions, which can enhance public safety by reducing drug resistance and improving outcomes. [11].

### Patient Summary
Being involved in treatment decisions helps you understand your care and supports your ability to stick to the treatment plan. [1]. Getting support and education about your treatment can help you follow through with it, which is important for your health and the health of others. [2]. Learning about tuberculosis and its treatment can help you understand what to expect and how to manage your health better. [4].

Your involvement in your care is important and can lead to better health outcomes. [5]. When you receive support during treatment, it can lead to better results and help prevent the disease from spreading. [11].

reference: Patient involvement in tuberculosis treatment is essential because it fosters adherence to the complex regimen that typically involves multiple drugs over an extended period. By engaging patients in decision-making regarding treatment supervision and overall care, healthcare providers can enhance adherence, which is critical for effective disease management and minimizing the risk of transmission. This collaborative approach not only respects the rights of the patient but also serves to protect public safety by ensuring that treatment is completed successfully.

response:
### Clinician Summary
If patients exposed to smear-positive TB are known to be particularly susceptible to infection, manage their TB risk in the same way as close contacts. [2]. If an inpatient with smear-positive TB is found to have multidrug-resistant TB, or if exposed patients are HIV positive, trace contacts following the Interdepartmental Working Group on Tuberculosis guidelines. [1].

### Patient Summary
If you have been exposed to someone with smear-positive TB and are particularly susceptible to infection, your TB risk should be managed like that of close contacts. [2].

If they are determined to be close contacts or are particularly susceptible to infection, their TB risk should be managed in the same manner as that of close contacts. This includes providing appropriate 'inform and advise' information and notifying their general practitioner.

user_input: What are the recommendations regarding the use of bedaquiline and delamanid in pediatric patients, particularly those aged under 6 years?
retrieved_contexts: ['No context retrieved.']
response:
**No Confidence — Abstaining**

Insufficient evidence to answer with confidence.
reference: The guidelines indicate that the risk-benefit considerations for bedaquiline in patients aged 6–17 years are similar to those for adults, although more data is needed before this recommendation can be upgraded to a strong one. For delamanid, the use is restricted to children aged 3–5 years based on safety and pharmacological data, as efficacy and safety extrapolations cannot be made for those under 3 years. No distinct safety signals were observed in children aged 3–5 years compared to adults.

response:
### Clinician Summary
Severe effects usually require the offending drug(s) to be discontinued. If a drug is permanently discontinued, then a replacement drug, typically from a different drug class, is included in the regimen. Patients with severe tuberculosis often require the initiation of an alternate regimen during the time the offending drug(s) are held. [1]. Consult a TB medical expert to assist in managing severe adverse reactions. [2].

### Patient Summary
If you experience severe side effects from your tuberculosis medication, your doctor may stop that medication and give you a different one. [1]. It's important to talk to a tuberculosis expert if you have severe side effects from your treatment. [2].

In cases where a drug is permanently discontinued, a replacement drug, typically from a different drug class, should be included in the treatment regimen. Additionally, patients may require the initiation of an alternate regimen while the offending drug is held.

user_input: What are the recommended antibiotic options for treating community-acquired pneumonia (CAP) in patients with documented allergies or contraindications to macrolides or fluoroquinolones?
retrieved_contexts: ['No context retrieved.']
response:
**No Confidence — Abstaining**

The retrieved evidence does not contain enough information to answer this question.
reference: In patients with documented allergies or contraindications to macrolides or fluoroquinolones, doxycycline is recommended as an alternative to a macrolide when used in combination with a beta-lactam as a third option. Additionally, omadacycline has been reported to be equivalent to moxifloxacin as monotherapy for adults with nonsevere CAP, particularly in cases of tetracycline resistance. However, due to limited safety data and the fact that it is based on a single published report, omadacycline is not currently listed as an alternative treatment option.

response:
### Clinician Summary
Use of intermittent tuberculosis treatment regimens in HIV-infected patients has been associated with high rates of relapse and the emergence of drug resistance. [1]. Relapse of tuberculosis in HIV-infected individuals is associated with non-adherence to treatment, use of intermittent regimens, and with low plasma drug concentrations, all of which also contribute to the emergence of rifamycin resistance. [4]. Experts believe that highly intermittent, once-weekly regimens should be avoided because of insufficient experience with this regimen in extrapulmonary tuberculosis. [7].

Treatment of HIV-related tuberculosis should be given daily in both the intensive and continuation phases to avoid recurrent disease and the emergence of rifamycin resistance. [10].

### Patient Summary
Using intermittent treatment for tuberculosis in people with HIV can lead to more relapses and the risk of the bacteria becoming resistant to drugs. [1]. Not sticking to the treatment plan and using intermittent regimens can cause tuberculosis to come back and make it harder to treat. [4]. Doctors recommend avoiding very infrequent treatment schedules because they haven't been tested enough for safety and effectiveness. [7].

To prevent the disease from coming back and to stop the bacteria from becoming resistant, people with HIV and tuberculosis should take their medications every day. [10].

### References
1. KB: Existing KB - Guidelines, Document: ATS CDC IDSA Drug-Susceptible TB Treatment Guidelines 2016
reference: The use of intermittent tuberculosis treatment regimens in HIV-infected patients has been associated with high rates of relapse and the emergence of drug resistance. This suggests that such treatment strategies may not be optimal for this population, highlighting the need for careful consideration of treatment regimens in patients co-infected with HIV.

### Clinician Summary
The recommended duration of tuberculosis treatment for solid organ transplant recipients is at least 9 months. [1]. The management of immunosuppressive therapy in patients who develop active tuberculosis varies based on the comorbid condition, with steps taken to correct immunodeficiency if possible. [1].

### Patient Summary
If you have a solid organ transplant and get tuberculosis, you should be treated for at least 9 months. [1]. How your immune-suppressing medications are managed will depend on your other health conditions. [1].

The management of immunosuppressive therapy in patients who develop active tuberculosis is tailored based on the specific comorbid conditions of the patient, and efforts are made to correct any underlying immunodeficiency if feasible.

response:
### Clinician Summary
Commissioners in rural areas should consider collaborative approaches to deliver and manage TB services. [1]. Factors to consider include local TB incidence, local at-risk populations and their movements, existing service configurations, and the need to share services across different geographical areas. [3]. The composition and capacity of the local multidisciplinary TB team should be organized based on local audit results and the location of services. [2].

Social and cultural barriers to accessing health services, such as fear of stigma and staff attitudes, should be addressed. [4]. Local referral pathways should be established, detailing who to refer and how. [4]. Voluntary organizations working with at-risk groups should be involved in developing appropriate TB referral pathways. [6]. Multidisciplinary TB teams should accept referrals from healthcare providers and allied organizations working with under-served groups. [6].

### Patient Summary
Local TB services should work together to help people at risk. [1]. It's important to know how many people have TB in the area and who is most at risk. [3]. The team that helps with TB should be well-organized and know where to provide services. [2].

People may be afraid to seek help because of stigma, so it's important to make them feel safe. [4]. There should be clear ways for people to get referred to TB services. [4]. Organizations that help at-risk groups should know how to connect people to TB services. [6].

reference: When organizing local tuberculosis (TB) services for at-risk groups, it is essential to consider indices of social deprivation, as these can impact the health outcomes of the population. Additionally, collaboration with local statutory and non-statutory services that work with these groups is crucial to ensure comprehensive care. The composition and capacity of the local multidisciplinary TB team should also be evaluated to determine the adequacy of resources and expertise available for effective TB management.

### Clinician Summary
Monitor coagulation tests during rifampin treatment in patients at risk of vitamin K deficiency. [1]. Consider discontinuation of Rifampin if abnormal coagulation tests and/or bleeding occur. [1]. Advise patients to abstain from alcohol, hepatotoxic medications or herbal products while taking rifampin. [2].

Avoid concomitant use with atazanavir, darunavir, fosamprenavir, saquinavir, or tipranavir due to the potential of rifampin to substantially decrease plasma concentrations of these antiviral drugs. [5].

### Patient Summary
You should have your blood tested regularly while on rifampin to check for any issues with blood clotting. [1]. It's important to avoid alcohol and certain medications while taking rifampin. [2]. Do not take rifampin with specific HIV medications like atazanavir or saquinavir, as it can make them less effective. [5].

reference: When coadministering antiretroviral therapy, particularly nevirapine or efavirenz, with rifampicin-based antitubercular therapy, it is crucial to monitor for potential drug interactions that may affect the efficacy of either treatment. Rifampicin is a potent inducer of cytochrome P450 enzymes, which can lead to decreased plasma concentrations of antiretroviral agents, necessitating dose adjustments. Clinicians should also assess the patient's clinical response and side effects closely to ensure effective management of both tuberculosis and HIV.

### Clinician Summary
Isoniazid is known to slow the metabolism of carbamazepine and increase its serum levels. [1]. Carbamazepine levels should be determined prior to concurrent administration with isoniazid. [1]. Signs and symptoms of carbamazepine toxicity should be monitored closely. [1].

### Patient Summary
Isoniazid can make carbamazepine stay in your body longer, which may increase the risk of side effects. [1]. Your doctor will check your carbamazepine levels before starting isoniazid. [1]. It's important to watch for signs of carbamazepine side effects while taking isoniazid. [1].

Conversely, carbamazepine may also affect the metabolism of isoniazid, potentially reducing its efficacy. Clinicians should be vigilant in managing these interactions to ensure effective tuberculosis treatment while minimizing the risk of adverse effects.

response:
### Clinician Summary
The analysis showed that in longer MDR-TB treatment regimens, the risk of treatment failure, relapse and death was comparable when the treatment started with four, five or six medicines likely to be effective. [1]. It would be desirable to give patients the minimum number of medicines necessary to obtain comparable levels of relapse-free cure, considering drug–drug interactions, pill burden and likelihood of adverse events. [1]. All three Group A agents and at least one Group B agent should be included to ensure that treatment starts with at least four TB agents likely to be effective. [2].

If only one or two Group A agents are used, both Group B agents are to be included. [2]. If the regimen cannot be composed with agents from Groups A and B alone, Group C agents are added to complete it. [2]. Starting with five effective agents rather than four may be advisable if there are health risks requiring early cessation of both bedaquiline and linezolid. [8].

### Patient Summary
When treating MDR-TB, starting with four, five, or six effective medicines has similar risks of treatment failure, relapse, and death. [1]. It's better to use the fewest medicines necessary to avoid complications like drug interactions and side effects. [1]. At least four effective TB medicines should be used at the start of treatment, including specific groups of medicines. [2].

If fewer than three Group A medicines are used, then both Group B medicines should be included. [2]. If the treatment can't be made with the first two groups of medicines, then other medicines can be added. [2]. In some cases, using five effective medicines instead of four might be safer for patients with certain health risks. [8].

### References
1. KB: Existing KB - Guidelines, Document: Unknown
reference: When determining the minimum number of agents for treating MDR-TB, it is essential to consider the risk of treatment failure, relapse, and death, which appears comparable when starting treatment with four, five, or six effective medicines. Additionally, the analysis suggests that using three agents in the continuation phase does not adversely affect outcomes compared to four agents. Therefore, minimizing the number of medications is desirable to reduce drug–drug interactions, pill burden, and the likelihood of adverse events, while still achieving comparable levels of relapse-free cure.

### Clinician Summary
Acid amplification tests for the M. tuberculosis complex should be performed if there is clinical suspicion of TB disease and the person has HIV. [1]. Acid amplification tests should also be performed if rapid information about mycobacterial species would alter the person's care. [1]. Additionally, these tests are indicated if the need for a large contact-tracing initiative is being explored. [1].

### Patient Summary
If a doctor thinks you might have TB and you have HIV, they will do a special test. [1]. They will also do this test if knowing the type of bacteria could change how you are treated. [1]. Finally, if they need to trace a lot of people you might have been in contact with, they will do this test. [1].

### Clinician Summary
The TB network local coordinator should work in close collaboration with clinicians and all relevant multidisciplinary TB teams to develop the network and be responsible for setting up the network and developing it based on needs, reporting back to the TB control board regularly, and establishing the links, partnerships and relationships across their local network. [1]. TB control boards should consider the need for a TB network local coordinator, particularly if working across multiple clinical commissioning group areas. [2].

### Patient Summary
The TB network local coordinator helps bring together doctors and teams to create and improve the TB network, ensuring it meets local needs and keeps the TB control board updated. [1]. The coordinator is important when there are multiple areas involved in TB care. [2].

### References
1. KB: Existing KB - Guidelines, Document: NICE TB Guidelines NG33 - Recommendations (UK 2024)
reference: The TB network local coordinator plays a crucial role in facilitating collaboration among various stakeholders involved in tuberculosis control efforts. This position is particularly important when working across multiple clinical commissioning group areas, as it helps ensure cohesive planning and implementation of TB control strategies. The coordinator's responsibilities may include coordinating resources, managing communication between different entities, and overseeing local TB initiatives to enhance overall effectiveness in managing and controlling tuberculosis.

response:
### Clinician Summary
At a minimum, patients suspected of having pulmonary tuberculosis have 2 sputum specimens (using sputum induction with hypertonic saline if necessary) for AFB smears and cultures for mycobacteria or for rapid molecular testing for M. [1]. Patients in whom pulmonary tuberculosis is suspected have 3 appropriate sputum specimens collected for microscopic examination and mycobacterial culture, and at least one specimen is tested with a rapid molecular test. [4]. Preferably, respiratory samples should include 3 deep cough sputum samples, with at least 1 early morning sample. [10].

Multiple respiratory samples should be sent for TB microscopy and culture. [11].

### Patient Summary
If a doctor thinks you might have tuberculosis, they will ask for at least 2 samples of your sputum, and sometimes they will use a special method to help you cough it up. [1]. They will also collect 3 samples of your sputum, and one of those samples will be tested quickly to check for tuberculosis. [4]. It's best to provide 3 samples of sputum that you cough up deeply, and one of those should be from the early morning. [10].

Doctors will send multiple samples of your sputum to check for tuberculosis. [11].

reference: When obtaining sputum specimens for the diagnosis of tuberculosis (TB), it is essential to ensure the quality of the sputum produced, as this directly affects the reliability of laboratory tests. Care should be taken to collect adequate specimens and transport them to the laboratory following standard procedures to maintain the viability of the bacilli. Additionally, the availability of quality-assured laboratories capable of performing TB culture is crucial, as some countries face challenges in implementing and assuring the quality of sputum culture.

supervision must decide whether to restart a complete course
of treatment or simply to continue as intended originally.', 'Tuberculosis Clinical Care for Children There are special considerations for tuberculosis testing and treatment in children. Tuberculosis Clinical Care and Treatment During Pregnancy

response:
### Clinician Summary
A break in the prescribed anti-TB regimen for 2 weeks or more in the initial phase, or more than 20% of prescribed doses missed intermittently, is considered a treatment interruption. [1]. When interruptions occur, the person responsible for supervision must decide whether to restart a complete course of treatment or simply to continue as intended originally. [8]. Bacteriologic status of the patient prior to and after the interruption are also important considerations. [5].

If interruptions are due to an interim loss of follow-up, additional sputum should be obtained for repeat culture and drug susceptibility testing when the patient is returned to treatment. [4]. In people who have experienced a treatment interruption because of drug-induced hepatotoxicity, it is important to investigate other causes of acute liver reactions and wait until liver function tests return to normal before reintroducing anti-TB drugs. [3].

### Patient Summary
If you miss your TB treatment for 2 weeks or more, or if you miss a lot of doses, it's important to talk to your healthcare provider about what to do next. [1]. Your doctor will decide if you need to start your treatment over or if you can continue from where you left off. [8]. It's important for your doctor to check your health before and after any breaks in your treatment. [5].

If you have missed treatment because you weren't able to follow up, your doctor will need to do more tests when you come back. [4]. If you had a break in treatment because of liver problems, your doctor will check your liver health before restarting your TB medications. [3].

reference: When interruptions in therapy for tuberculosis occur, the supervising clinician must evaluate the extent of the interruption and the patient's clinical status. The decision to either restart a complete course of treatment or to continue with the originally intended regimen should be based on factors such as the duration of the interruption, the patient's adherence history, and the presence of any immunosuppressing conditions. It is crucial to ensure that the treatment plan effectively addresses the risk of drug resistance and disease progression.

Factors to consider when deciding to prolong treatment in patients with either cavitation or a positive culture at 2 months include being >10% below ideal body weight; being an active smoker; having diabetes, HIV infection, or any immunosuppressing condition; or having extensive disease on chest radiograph. [2]. Patients with either cavitation or a positive culture at 2 months are followed more closely and consideration is given to extending treatment duration if there are suggestions of a poor response. [3].

### Patient Summary
When deciding to extend tuberculosis treatment, doctors consider if the patient is significantly underweight, a smoker, has diabetes or HIV, or has extensive lung disease. [2]. Patients with certain risk factors are monitored more closely, and treatment may be extended if they are not responding well. [3].

reference: Survivors of community-acquired pneumonia (CAP) face significant long-term health risks, including an increased incidence of cardiovascular events. Studies indicate that as many as 20% of CAP survivors experience cardiovascular complications. Furthermore, the mortality risk for these individuals can be as high as 30% within 2 to 5 years following the initial CAP episode.

Patients are eligible for the BPaL regimen if they have not received bedaquiline or linezolid for 2 weeks or more previously, and this was an eligibility criterion of the Nix-TB study. [2]. It is important to provide effective patient support to enable adherence to treatment, and close monitoring for adverse events, response to treatment and emerging drug resistance. [1]. Patients who receive BPaL need to be tested at baseline and then monitored during treatment using schedules of relevant clinical and laboratory testing. [5].

Children and pregnant women were ineligible for inclusion in the Nix-TB study, and therefore will not be an eligible population for BPaL at the present time. [4]. A predefined schedule of clinical and microbiological monitoring is in place, preferably including post-treatment completion follow-up. [9].

### Patient Summary
You can only receive the BPaL treatment if you haven't taken certain medications for at least 2 weeks. [2]. It's important to have support during treatment and to be closely watched for any side effects or issues with the treatment. [1]. You will need to have tests done before starting treatment and during it to check your health. [5].

If you are a child or pregnant, you cannot receive the BPaL treatment right now. [4].

response:
### Clinician Summary
Patients with positive AFB smears but negative cultures may still have active pulmonary tuberculosis, as failure to isolate M. tuberculosis does not exclude the diagnosis. [2]. AFB smear-positive but culture-negative results can occur, particularly in patients with far-advanced cavitary tuberculosis after the first few months of treatment, indicating that the presence of dead organisms is not a sign of treatment failure. [6]. In cases of culture-negative tuberculosis, appropriate diagnostic studies should be undertaken, and treatment may be initiated based on clinical and radiographic evaluation. [11].

### Patient Summary
Even if tests show positive AFB smears but negative cultures, a patient may still have active tuberculosis. [2]. Sometimes, patients with advanced tuberculosis can have positive smear results but negative cultures because the bacteria may be dead, which doesn't mean the treatment isn't working. [6]. Doctors will do further tests and may start treatment for tuberculosis based on the patient's symptoms and imaging, even if initial tests are negative. [11].

### References
1. KB: Existing KB - Guidelines, Document: ATS CDC IDSA Drug-Susceptible TB Treatment Guidelines 2016
reference: The presence of positive acid-fast bacilli (AFB) smears alongside negative cultures in a patient evaluated for pulmonary tuberculosis may indicate several clinical scenarios. These include the possibility of non-viable organisms being detected in the smear, or the patient may have a form of TB that is difficult to culture. This situation necessitates careful clinical correlation and may require additional diagnostic testing to confirm active disease and guide appropriate treatment.

response:
### Clinician Summary
The stages of pathological changes in typical bacterial lobar pneumonia progress from congestion with alveolar edema to red hepatization with a hemorrhagic alveolar exudate, and then to grey hepatization characterized by a fibrinopurulent exudate. [1].

### Patient Summary
In typical bacterial lobar pneumonia, the disease starts with swelling and fluid in the lungs, then progresses to a stage where the lungs become red and filled with blood, and finally, it changes to a grey stage where pus is present. [1].

### References
1. KB: Existing KB - Guidelines, Document: Medscape CAP Clinical Practice Guidelines
2. KB: Existing KB - Guidelines, Document: WHO Pneumonia Fact Sheet
reference: The pathology of typical bacterial lobar pneumonia progresses through several distinct stages. Initially, there is congestion with alveolar edema. This is followed by red hepatization, characterized by a hemorrhagic alveolar exudate. Finally, the process transitions to grey hepatization, which is marked by a fibrinopurulent exudate resulting from the breakdown of red blood cells.

response:
### Clinician Summary
TB blood tests measure the immune response to TB proteins in whole blood to determine if a person is infected with TB bacteria. For TB blood tests, specimens are mixed with peptides that simulate antigens derived from TB bacteria and with controls. In most people infected with TB bacteria, the white blood cells recognize the simulated antigens and release interferon-gamma (IFN-γ). The tests measure the level of IFN-γ response. [1]. A positive reaction to a TB blood test is interpreted as TB infection. More tests, such as a chest radiograph, are needed to rule out TB disease. [5]. A diagnosis of latent TB infection is made if a person has a positive TB blood test or TB skin test result and a medical exam does not indicate TB disease. [4].

### Patient Summary
TB blood tests check how your immune system responds to proteins from the TB bacteria. If your immune cells react and produce a substance called interferon-gamma, it suggests you might be infected with TB. [1]. If the TB blood test is positive, it means you likely have a TB infection, but further tests are needed to confirm if you have active TB disease. [5]. If you test positive for TB but do not show signs of active disease, you may be diagnosed with latent TB infection. [4].

### References
1. KB: Existing KB - Guidelines, Document: Overview Latent Tb
2. KB: Existing KB - Guidelines, Document: CDC TB Clinical Guidelines Overview
3. KB: Existing KB - Guidelines, Document: Unknown
4. KB: Existing KB - Guidelines, Document: Treatment Latent Tb
reference: TB blood tests are designed to detect the immune response to TB proteins in whole blood. When a person is infected with TB bacteria, their white blood cells recognize specific antigens derived from the bacteria and subsequently release interferon-gamma (IFN-γ). The tests measure the level of IFN-γ response, which indicates whether the individual has been infected with TB.

response:
### Clinician Summary
The use of a thrice-weekly rifampin-based regimen during the intensive phase was associated with a higher rate of relapse and emergence of resistance. [1]. Thrice-weekly therapy in the intensive phase may be considered for patients who are not HIV-infected and are at low risk of relapse. [2]. In the continuation phase, thrice-weekly therapy is suggested instead of twice-weekly therapy if intermittent therapy is administered. [3].

Daily or thrice-weekly dosing is recommended for the continuation phase of therapy. [4]. Thrice-weekly therapy is recommended for the continuation phase based on systematic reviews. [5].

### Patient Summary
Using a thrice-weekly rifampin-based regimen during the intensive phase may lead to a higher chance of relapse. [1]. Patients who are not HIV-infected and have a low risk of relapse may consider thrice-weekly therapy during the intensive phase. [2]. In the continuation phase, thrice-weekly therapy is preferred over twice-weekly therapy if intermittent therapy is used. [3].

For the continuation phase, daily or thrice-weekly dosing is recommended. [4]. Thrice-weekly therapy is supported for the continuation phase based on evidence reviews. [5].

### References
1. KB: Existing KB - Guidelines, Document: ATS CDC IDSA Drug-Susceptible TB Treatment Guidelines 2016
2. KB: Existing KB - Guidelines, Document: Treatment Active Tb
reference: The use of a thrice-weekly rifampin-based regimen during both the intensive and continuation phases of tuberculosis treatment has been associated with a higher rate of relapse and the emergence of rifamycin resistance. This suggests that such a dosing schedule may compromise treatment efficacy and increase the risk of developing drug-resistant strains of Mycobacterium tuberculosis, highlighting the importance of adherence to recommended dosing regimens to ensure optimal treatment outcomes.

response:
### Clinician Summary
Multidisciplinary TB teams should work in partnership with voluntary, community and statutory organisations to conduct outreach contact investigations. [1]. Multidisciplinary TB teams should encourage peer educators or TB programme support workers to help with contact investigations involving under-served people who have complex social networks. [1]. Multidisciplinary TB teams should provide or arrange outreach services to ensure contact investigations can be arranged in the community. [2].

Multidisciplinary TB teams should coordinate contact investigations at places where the person with TB spends significant amounts of time, particularly in settings identified by looking at social networks. [5][3]. Multidisciplinary TB teams should work alongside health and social care professionals known to them to help trace relevant contacts in under-served groups. [6]. Multidisciplinary TB teams should work in partnership with voluntary organisations and 'community champions' to increase awareness of TB among under-served groups at risk of infection. [9].

### Patient Summary
TB teams work with community groups to find and help people who may have been in contact with TB. [1]. They help connect people with support workers to assist in finding others who might be affected. [1]. TB teams make sure that tests and investigations can happen in the community where people live. [2].

They look for people in places where those with TB spend time, especially in communities where people may not have easy access to healthcare. [5][3]. TB teams collaborate with local health workers to trace contacts in communities that need extra help. [6]. They also partner with local organizations to raise awareness about TB in at-risk communities. [9].

### References
1. KB: Existing KB - Guidelines, Document: NICE TB Guidelines NG33 - Recommendations (UK 2024)
reference: Multidisciplinary TB teams are encouraged to collaborate with peer educators or TB programme support workers to facilitate outreach contact investigations. This approach is particularly important for engaging underserved populations who may have complex social networks, ensuring that contact tracing and support are effectively tailored to their specific needs.

response:
### Clinician Summary
Patients with chronic renal failure have worse clinical outcomes during anti-tuberculosis treatment compared to those without renal failure, necessitating close monitoring during treatment [2]. The pharmacokinetics of antituberculosis drugs are altered in patients with renal insufficiency or end-stage renal disease, which may require dose adjustments [3].

### Patient Summary
If you have chronic kidney problems, your treatment for tuberculosis might not work as well, and doctors will need to keep a close eye on your progress [2]. The way your body processes tuberculosis medications changes if you have kidney issues, so your doctor might need to adjust your medication doses [3].

### References
1. KB: Existing KB - Guidelines, Document: ATS CDC IDSA Drug-Susceptible TB Treatment Guidelines 2016
2. KB: Existing KB - Guidelines, Document: Unknown
reference: Chronic renal failure can significantly affect the pharmacokinetics of anti-tuberculosis medications, leading to altered drug levels and potential treatment failure. Patients with renal impairment may require dose adjustments and closer monitoring to avoid toxicity and ensure therapeutic efficacy. It is crucial for clinicians to consider renal function when prescribing anti-tuberculosis regimens to optimize treatment outcomes.

### Clinician Summary
The IDSA guidelines for CAP include ceftaroline as part of empiric beta-lactam therapy for inpatients, and it is often reserved for patients who have risk factors for MRSA as it is also active against MRSA. ### Patient Summary
Ceftaroline is used in the treatment of pneumonia for hospitalized patients, especially if they are at risk for a specific type of bacteria called MRSA. [4].

However, it is typically reserved for patients who have risk factors for methicillin-resistant Staphylococcus aureus (MRSA), as ceftaroline demonstrates activity against MRSA.

response:
### Clinician Summary
Consider the use of an expanded empiric regimen in consultation with experts in the treatment of drug-resistant disease when immediate treatment initiation is necessary. [1]. Immediate referral to, or consultation with a specialty center is indicated for management of drug-resistant tuberculosis. [4]. If treatment failure is presumably due to drug resistance and the patient is seriously ill or has a positive sputum AFB smear, an empiric regimen is started immediately and continued until susceptibility tests are available to guide therapy. [4].

Treatment regimens should include only drugs to which the Mycobacterium tuberculosis isolate have documented or high likelihood of susceptibility. [10]. Health care providers should review comorbidities, current medications, and the results of drug susceptibility tests to choose the appropriate medications for treating each patient. [11]. If clinicians are not familiar with the management of drug-resistant tuberculosis, immediate referral to, or consultation with a specialty center is indicated. [12].

### Patient Summary
When starting treatment for drug-resistant tuberculosis right away, doctors should work with specialists and consider using a broader range of medications. [1]. If a patient is very sick or has a positive test for tuberculosis, doctors will start treatment immediately and adjust it once they have more test results. [4]. Doctors will only use medications that are known to work against the specific strain of tuberculosis the patient has. [10].

Doctors will look at other health issues and current medications to decide the best treatment for the patient. [11].

### References
1. KB: Existing KB - Guidelines, Document: ATS CDC IDSA Drug-Susceptible TB Treatment Guidelines 2016
2. KB: Existing KB - Guidelines, Document: Unknown
3. KB: Existing KB - Guidelines, Document: NICE TB Guidelines NG33 - Recommendations (UK 2024)
4. KB: Existing KB - Guidelines, Document: Treatment Active Tb
reference: When immediate treatment initiation is necessary for drug-resistant tuberculosis, it is important to consider the use of an expanded empiric regimen. This decision should be made in consultation with experts in the treatment of drug-resistant disease to ensure that the selected regimen is appropriate and effective.

Drug susceptibility testing (DST) is crucial for confirming eligibility for different MDR/RR-TB treatment regimens, including the BPaL regimen, and for maximizing treatment effectiveness by testing the patient's MDR/RR-TB strain for susceptibility to planned medicines. ### Patient Summary
Testing for drug resistance helps doctors choose the best treatment for patients with multidrug-resistant TB, ensuring they receive the most effective medicines. [2]. Quick tests that can show if the TB bacteria are resistant to certain drugs are important for deciding the right treatment plan for patients. [8].

Furthermore, baseline DST confirms patient eligibility for various treatment options and underscores the need for enhanced access to reliable DST methods, including the use of rapid tests recommended by the WHO.

response:
### Clinician Summary
A 4-drug regimen of INH, RIF, PZA, and EMB remains the preferred initial treatment for drug-susceptible pulmonary tuberculosis. Treatment is initiated promptly even before AFB smear microscopy, molecular tests, and mycobacterial culture results are known in patients with high likelihood of having tuberculosis or those seriously ill with a disorder suspicious for tuberculosis. [1]. Patients who, on the basis of careful clinical and radiographic evaluation, are thought to have pulmonary tuberculosis should have treatment initiated with INH, RIF, PZA, and EMB even when the initial sputum smears are negative. [8].

### Patient Summary
The best initial treatment for drug-susceptible pulmonary tuberculosis is a combination of four medications: INH, RIF, PZA, and EMB. Treatment should start quickly, even before test results are available, if the doctor thinks you might have tuberculosis or if you are very ill. [1]. If doctors believe you have pulmonary tuberculosis based on your symptoms and medical tests, they will start treatment with INH, RIF, PZA, and EMB, even if your initial tests do not show tuberculosis. [8].

### References
1. KB: Existing KB - Guidelines, Document: ATS CDC IDSA Drug-Susceptible TB Treatment Guidelines 2016
reference: The preferred initial treatment regimen for drug-susceptible pulmonary tuberculosis includes isoniazid (INH), rifampin (RIF), pyrazinamide (PZA), and ethambutol (EMB). Treatment should be initiated promptly in patients who have a high likelihood of having tuberculosis or in those who are seriously ill with a condition that is suspicious for tuberculosis, even before the results of AFB smear microscopy, molecular tests, and mycobacterial culture are available.

response:
### Clinician Summary
Assess the risk of TB for a new healthcare worker who knows he or she is HIV-positive at the time of recruitment as part of the occupational health checks. [1]. The employer, through the occupational health department, should be aware of the settings with increased risk of exposure to TB, and that these pose increased risks to HIV-positive healthcare workers. [2]. Healthcare workers who are found to be HIV-positive during employment should have medical and occupational assessments of TB risk, and may need to modify their work to reduce exposure. [3].

### Patient Summary
When hiring a new healthcare worker who is HIV-positive, it's important to check their risk of tuberculosis (TB) during health assessments. [1]. Employers should know that certain work environments have a higher risk of TB exposure for HIV-positive workers. [2]. If a healthcare worker becomes HIV-positive while employed, they should have their TB risk evaluated and may need to change their job duties to stay safe. [3].

### References
1. KB: Existing KB - Guidelines, Document: NICE TB Guidelines NG33 - Recommendations (UK 2024)
2. KB: Existing KB - Guidelines, Document: CDC TB Clinical Guidelines Overview
reference: When assessing the risk of tuberculosis (TB) for a new healthcare worker who is HIV-positive, it is crucial to incorporate this information into the occupational health checks. HIV-positive individuals are at a higher risk for developing active TB due to their compromised immune system. Therefore, a thorough evaluation of their potential exposure to TB and appropriate preventive measures should be implemented to safeguard both the worker and the patients they may encounter.

response:
### Clinician Summary
Enhanced case management involves the management of TB for someone with clinically or socially complex needs, starting as soon as TB is suspected, and includes considering the need for directly observed treatment along with a package of supportive care tailored to the person's needs. [1]. It helps in overcoming barriers to completing diagnostic investigations and TB treatment, which may include issues related to transport, housing, nutrition, and immigration status. [2]. Enhanced case management is overseen by a case manager, typically a specialist TB nurse, and includes a collaborative, multidisciplinary approach that should start as soon as possible after a suspected case is discovered. [5].

The need for directly observed therapy is re-evaluated throughout the course of TB treatment whenever the person's circumstances change. [4].

### Patient Summary
Enhanced case management helps people with complex needs related to TB by providing support and treatment as soon as TB is suspected, including direct supervision of treatment and personalized care. [1]. It addresses challenges like transportation, housing, and nutrition that might make it hard for someone to get the TB tests and treatment they need. [2]. A case manager, usually a specialized nurse, coordinates the care and ensures that help starts quickly after TB is suspected. [5].

The treatment plan is adjusted as needed based on changes in the person's situation. [4].

### References
1. KB: Existing KB - Guidelines, Document: NICE TB Guidelines NG33 - Recommendations (UK 2024)
2. KB: Existing KB - Guidelines, Document: ATS CDC IDSA Drug-Susceptible TB Treatment Guidelines 2016
reference: Enhanced case management plays a crucial role in the treatment of tuberculosis (TB) for individuals with clinically or socially complex needs by initiating management as soon as TB is suspected. This approach includes considering the need for directly observed treatment (DOT) to ensure adherence to the medication regimen. Additionally, it involves providing a tailored package of supportive care that addresses the specific needs of the individual, thereby improving treatment outcomes and overall management of the disease.

Health care providers should review comorbidities, current medications, and the results of drug susceptibility tests to choose the appropriate medications for treating each patient. [1]. Treatment regimens should include only drugs to which the Mycobacterium tuberculosis isolate have documented or high likelihood of susceptibility. [2].

A patient's treatment regimen may depend on factors including drug-drug interactions, comorbidities, patient preferences, harms and benefits associated with the drugs, ability to appropriately monitor for adverse effects, and drug availability. [2]. Consult with a TB expert for assistance managing a patient with drug-resistant TB disease. [2]. Health care providers should assess the patient's progress at least monthly, including clinical monitoring, laboratory testing, and patient education. [8].

Your doctor will work with TB experts to find the best treatment for your drug-resistant TB. [1]. Your treatment will consider other health issues you have, the medications you are taking, and how your TB responds to certain drugs. [1]. The medications you receive will only be those that are effective against your specific strain of TB. [2].

Your treatment plan will take into account how the medications might interact with each other and your personal preferences. [2]. Your doctor will check on your health regularly to make sure the treatment is working and to help you with any side effects. [8].

### Clinician Summary
Key considerations when developing a case management plan include improving 'treatment literacy' by educating the patient about tuberculosis and its treatment, including possible adverse effects; discussing expected outcomes of treatment, specifically the ability to cure the patient of the disease; reviewing methods of supervision and assessing response to therapy; and discussing infectiousness and infection control measures using terminology that is appropriate to the culture, language, age, and reading level of the patient. [2].

### Patient Summary
It's important to understand your treatment and its effects, know what to expect from it, and discuss how to prevent spreading the disease. [2]. Special care is needed for children, pregnant women, and people with HIV when treating tuberculosis. [4].

reference: The diagnosis of tuberculosis (TB) typically involves a combination of clinical evaluation, radiographic imaging, and microbiological testing, including sputum smear and culture for Mycobacterium tuberculosis. Management of TB requires a multi-drug regimen, often including isoniazid, rifampin, pyrazinamide, and ethambutol, administered for a minimum of six months. Monitoring for treatment adherence, potential drug resistance, and adverse effects is crucial, as well as ensuring appropriate follow-up to assess treatment efficacy and prevent transmission.
